sources.notes: Read the complete grouping and scoring rules, all examples, and the limits t <= 1e4, n <= 2e5, k <= 1e9, with sum n <= 2e5.
Read the official round editorial section and reference implementation; confirmed computing positive suffix balances and greedily taking the largest ones.
Actually read the full writeup; it independently derives that every cut has a fixed suffix-balance contribution and sorts those contributions in descending order.
Re-derived the cut-contribution identity by exchanging the sums over fish and cuts, then exhaustively matched the greedy result against all cut subsets for every binary string of length 2 through 8.

annotation.summary: Split the ordered fish into the minimum number of nonempty contiguous groups so that Bob's total group-index score exceeds Alice's by at least k, or report that this is impossible.
annotation.constraints: There are up to 1e4 test cases; 2 <= n <= 2e5, 1 <= k <= 1e9, and the sum of n is at most 2e5. There are n-1 possible cut positions, so enumerating partitions is impossible. A direct accumulated score can be Theta(n^2), so 64-bit accumulation is appropriate.
annotation.core_idea: Let w_i be +1 for a Bob fish and -1 for an Alice fish. A partition is determined by its chosen cuts after positions c in {1,...,n-1}; fish p receives one extra point for every chosen cut c<p. Therefore Bob's score minus Alice's score equals the sum, over chosen cuts c, of delta_c=sum_{p=c+1}^n w_p. These suffix-balance contributions are independent because every subset of cut positions is a valid partition. For any fixed number q of cuts, the maximum attainable difference is the sum of the q largest delta_c, so discard nonpositive contributions, sort the positive ones descending, and take the shortest prefix whose sum reaches k. The answer is q+1 groups; if all positive contributions sum to less than k, answer -1.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case; O(sum n log(max n)) time over the input.
tricks: Represent a partition only by its cut positions
Exchange the order of summation so each cut contributes one independent suffix balance
Use the q largest positive contributions to maximize the score difference for exactly q cuts
Add one to the selected cut count to recover the number of groups
verification_notes: The contribution formula was checked directly against the original group-index scoring definition.
Exhaustive comparison covered every binary string of length 2 through 8 and every k from 1 through n^2+1.
No unresolved technical or source uncertainty remains.
main_agent_review_notes: All proposed tags already exist in the live taxonomy; no candidate tag or translation is needed.
The official greedy tag was retained only after independently deriving the exchange argument.

solution_variants.name: suffix-cut-contributions-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Scan right-to-left to compute the Bob-minus-Alice balance of every suffix beginning after a legal cut. Sort the positive balances descending and select them until their sum reaches k; selected cuts plus one is the minimum group count.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case.

tags.evidence: The decisive reduction assigns every possible cut c the independent contribution delta_c equal to the Bob-minus-Alice balance of the suffix after c; the total score difference is exactly the sum of selected cut contributions.

For any q cuts, an exchange replacing a selected smaller contribution by an unselected larger one cannot decrease the score, so the minimum feasible q is the first descending prefix whose sum reaches k.
Sorting the positive suffix contributions in descending order exposes the best total for every possible cut count as a prefix sum.
A reverse cumulative sum computes the contribution of all n-1 cut positions in linear time instead of rescanning each suffix.
